coredata.dc:title: Self-propelled cellular translocation of Janus-shaped graphene quantum dots: A molecular dynamics simulation and thermodynamic analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Xianyu
Liu, Hongchao
Duan, Xianli
Hu, Qi
Liang, Kezhong
Li, Tingzhen
Zhao, Shuangliang
Liu, Honglai
coredata.dc:description: Although spatially heterogeneous oxidation is pivotal to the cellular translocation process of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the dynamical road to the thermodynamically stable translocation conformation remains hazy. In this study, we illuminate the bio-interface doctrine involving the cellular translocation of Janus-shaped oxidized GQDs by performing multi-dimensional pathway computations predicated on coupled microsecond-long molecular dynamics and well-tempered metadynamics simulations. The oxidation nanodomains of Janus-shaped GQDs spontaneously yield hydrogen-bonds with polar lipid heads, while their pristine graphene nanodomains hydrophobically associate with nonpolar lipid tails, and thus respond as self-propelled nanomotors across biological membranes. Hydrophobic-associations and hydrogen-bondings are found to be competitively coordinated, which dictates the dynamic pathway and thermodynamic states for cellular translocation, particularly modulating the adaptive self-propelling activity. The translocational phase diagram is drawn, demonstrating that GQDs with moderately square-patterned oxidation and thin thickness facilitate excellent translocation and minimal biological nanotoxicity. Importantly, intermolecular mechanochemistry is discovered to play a crucial role in energy transduction and nanomechanical interactions, which in turn affect the spatial rotation pathways of GQDs, as well as the membrane perturbations. Overall, this study provides an intuitive insight into the dynamics and thermodynamics of spontaneously translocating GQDs, opening up brand-new avenues for developing GQD-enabled nanocarriers or antibiotics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transmembrane
Janus carbon quantum dot
Molecular simulations
Thermodynamics
Spatial heterogeneity
Adaptive self-propulsion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Intrinsic equilibrium translation of single-layered GQDs
Origin of cellular translocation at a molecular level
Thermodynamics comparisons and adaptive self-propelling translocation
Translocation pathway and cytotoxicity of multi-layered GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi Science and Technology Base and Talent Project
Science and Technology Innovation Program of Wanzhou
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22108022 , and 21878078 ), and the Guangxi Science and Technology Base and Talent Project (AD21220017), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0005), the Chongqing Doctoral “Through Train” Scientific Research Project (No. CSTB2022BSXM-JCX0133), and the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Wanzhou (No. wzstc20210310).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experiment-derived and design-imitated molecular structure of Janus-shaped GQDs: (A1) AFM image, (A2-A3) and corresponding height profiles within two specified samples[22], (B1) triangular-oxidized, and (B2) square-oxidized GQDs. In these snapshots, carbon atoms are shown as cyan balls, oxygen atoms as red balls, and hydrogen atoms as white balls. As shown by the auxiliary line, hydrophilic (oxidation) and hydrophobic (graphene) nanodomains are separated completely. (A1-A3) Reproduced with permission from ref 22. Copyright 2020 Elsevier. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Molecular insights elucidate the transmembrane dynamics of GQDs by presenting (A1-A4) representative snapshots of cellular translocation states, (B) time evolution of the center of mass of the distance between GQDs and biological membranes, (C) time evolution of contact number between biological membranes and GQDs, and the number of hydrogen-bonds for (D1) triangular-oxidized and (D2) square-oxidized GQDs interacting with a biological membrane, and (E1) distance and (E2) angle probability distribution of hydrogen-bonds formed by GQDs and biological membranes. In the snapshots of POPC, nitrogen atoms are shown in blue, while phosphorus atoms are shown in tan. The color illustration of GQDs is the same as POPC, except for the carbon atoms in GQDs, which are shown as olivine colors to distinguish from POPC. The inset graphics of Figure B display the two-dimensional number density maps of various transmembrane states. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Thermodynamics and migration peculiarities of different triangular-oxidized GQDs: (A-C) two-dimensional free-energy surface for the penetration of triangular-oxidized GQDs through a biological membrane, (D-F) corresponding self-part of the van Hove correlation function results. The unit of free-energy is kJ/mol. Oxidation level increases from left to right. For the free-energy surface, those CVs define the mass center of the distance between a triangular-oxidized nanodomain or pure graphene domain and a biological membrane.

Thermodynamics and migration peculiarities of the different square-oxidized GQDs: (A-C) two-dimensional free-energy surface for the penetration of square-oxidized GQDs through a biological membrane, (D-F) corresponding self-part of the van Hove correlation function results. Oxidation level increases from left to right. For the free-energy surface, those variables define the mass center of the distance between square-oxidized nanodomains or pure graphene nanodomains and a biological membrane.

Comparison analysis of transmembrane pathways between spatially heterogeneous oxidized GQDs: free-energy profiles for (A1-A2) triangular-oxidized and (B1-B2) square-oxidized GQDs, and (C) corresponding box-plot distributions of solvent-accessible surface areas for single-layered GQDs, and typical trajectories of relatively spatial mobile for oxidized nanodomains and hydrophobic nanodomains for (D1-D2) triangular-oxidized and (D3-D4) square-oxidized GQDs. The color bar of the trajectory represents simulation time in units of µs.

Translocational pathway of different spatial oxidated GQDs: (A) phase diagram of translocational states illustrates the interrelationship between spatial oxidation pattern, oxidation level, and nanoflake thickness in determining equilibrium states of GQDs interacting with biological membranes; (B) the corresponding representative translocational states, and (C1) mass density profiles and (C2) electrostatic potentials of POPC membrane with GQDs along with Z-direction, (D1-D4) two-dimensional number density distribution and (E1-E4) pressure tensor of biological membranes which corresponds to different translocational states shown in Figure B. In Figure A, the red, green, and blue colors represent the typical states of lateral insertion, vertical insertion, and active binding, respectively. Various shading regions mark the approximate regions of these states. The unit of two-dimensional number density is nm−2, whereas the pressure tensor unit with the Z-direction is mN/m. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Self-propelled cellular translocation of Janus-shaped graphene quantum dots: A molecular dynamics simulation and thermodynamic analysis

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Water Environment Evolution and Pollution Control in Three Gorges Reservoir
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although spatially heterogeneous oxidation is pivotal to the cellular translocation process of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the dynamical road to the thermodynamically stable translocation conformation remains hazy. In this study, we illuminate the bio-interface doctrine involving the cellular translocation of Janus-shaped oxidized GQDs by performing multi-dimensional pathway computations predicated on coupled microsecond-long molecular dynamics and well-tempered metadynamics simulations. The oxidation nanodomains of Janus-shaped GQDs spontaneously yield hydrogen-bonds with polar lipid heads, while their pristine graphene nanodomains hydrophobically associate with nonpolar lipid tails, and thus respond as self-propelled nanomotors across biological membranes. Hydrophobic-associations and hydrogen-bondings are found to be competitively coordinated, which dictates the dynamic pathway and thermodynamic states for cellular translocation, particularly modulating the adaptive self-propelling activity. The translocational phase diagram is drawn, demonstrating that GQDs with moderately square-patterned oxidation and thin thickness facilitate excellent translocation and minimal biological nanotoxicity. Importantly, intermolecular mechanochemistry is discovered to play a crucial role in energy transduction and nanomechanical interactions, which in turn affect the spatial rotation pathways of GQDs, as well as the membrane perturbations. Overall, this study provides an intuitive insight into the dynamics and thermodynamics of spontaneously translocating GQDs, opening up brand-new avenues for developing GQD-enabled nanocarriers or antibiotics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their outstanding properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with diverse oxidized structures hold increasing promise in a variety of biological applications, including biosensors, enzyme immobilization, delivery nanocarriers, and tissue engineering[1–5]. Therefore, to minimize the side effects of oxidized GQD-based nanomaterials as well as to provide crucial guidance for tailoring their biological outcome, a fundamental elaboration of the bio-interface interactions between oxidized GQDs and cellular membranes is very essential[6,7].

Intermolecular interactions of oxidized GQDs with biological membranes have been extensively studied, including the examinations of the membrane composition[8–10], and various structural oxidized GQDs (i.e., oxidation[11,12], size[13,14], shape[15,16], nanoflake thickness[17,18]). Notably, Yan and Zerbetto et al. [6,19,20] have studied how heterogeneously oxidized GQD patterns interact with lipid bilayer membranes. GQDs with various oxidized patterns, such as square-shaped GQDs, edge-oxidized GQDs, and sparsely and densely oxidized GQDs, were studied in depth, and the relationships were established between these heterogeneous oxidized GQDs and their cytotoxicity as well as antibacterial activity. From a perspective of biosafety and cytotoxicity, hydrophobic/hydrophilic moieties in oxidized GQDs are not only responsible for the GQD-membrane interaction melodies, but also for their penetration pathways, as a result, leading to different degrees of irregular lipid rearrangement and membrane damage[19,21]. In general, oxidized GQDs possess both hydrophobic and hydrophilic nanodomains in their molecular architectures. The hydrophobic-hydrophilic balance emerges[22], which is the design paradigm for oxidized GQD design[11,19]. Specifically, the hydrophobic domains allow deep penetration but damage cellular membranes, whereas hydrophilic domains expedite active-accumulation from solution to the membrane surface and even delay and fail to embed into the membrane[11,17]. Therefore, it is possible that altering the hydrophobic-hydrophilic balance of GQDs may be an important route in legislating their interactions with biological membranes.

Recent studies have revealed that utilizing a spatially heterogeneous oxidation strategy is a dependable and direct method for regulating the hydrophobic-hydrophilic balance of GQDs. For example, Li et al. [23] studied the biological membrane-graphene nanocone interactions for elucidating the circular and radial hydrophilic patterned effects. Intriguingly, it was revealed that the vertical patterns on GQDs may rupture the cellular membrane while the horizontal patterns on GQDs can shift the preferred orientation in the penetration process. Similarly, Ji et al. [24] explored the oxidation location effects of GQDs on transmembrane behaviors. Compared to pristine GQDs, partially edge-oxidized GQDs, corner oxidized GQDs, and edge-oxidized GQDs, they found that heterogeneous oxidized patches are crucial for transmembrane pores. Importantly, they proposed a co-regulatory mechanism based on lipid extraction and sharp edge piercing, implying that, contrary to previous findings, membrane damage is caused by the oxidized GQD region rather than the hydrophobic region.[11,17,19]. Inspired by the literature[20,23,24], spatially heterogeneous oxidation also plays a pivotal role in the translocation dynamics and thermodynamics of heterogeneously oxidized GQDs. There is, however, an extremely limited comprehension of the mechanistic aspects of these interactions, particularly on the intermolecular interactions of Janus-shaped GQDs with biological membranes.

In this work, we shed light on some hitherto unknown impacts of Janus-shaped GQDs with various spatially heterogeneous oxidation on their interactions with biological membranes. Different from the conventional oxidized GQDs, Janus-shaped GQDs are distinguished at their separated hydrophobic/hydrophilic nanodomains along the graphene plane, generating the Janus-shaped topological architecture. According to Fig. 1 A1-A3, it was experimentally discovered that Janus-shaped GQDs exhibit gradient nanosheet heights along their graphene planes[22]. This remarkable architecture endows GQDs with physicochemical features similar to those of peptides[25] and amphiphilic polymers[26], which may lower the free-energy barrier of cellular translocation and prevent membrane disruption. Besides, the molecular simulation technique is a complementary tool for experimental studies since it inherently provides spatiotemporal resolutions to capture the dynamical details at the atomic scale[11,14,27,28]. Herein, microsecond-long molecular dynamics (MD) simulations and well-tempered metadynamics (WTmetaD) simulations are hierarchically incorporated to comprehensively study the dynamic pathway and thermodynamic properties of Janus-shaped GQDs as they spontaneously translocate through the biological membranes. GQDs, in particular, operate as automated nanomotors and display the adaptive self-propelling activity in the observed translocation process.

The detailed structure properties of Janus-shaped GQDs are presented in Fig. 1B1-B2, which display the triangular-oxidized and square-oxidized GQDs, respectively. They have a size of GQDs 4.5 × 2.4 nm2, with oxidation levels ranging from 7.21 % to 15.29 %, and nanoflake thickness less than 1.02 nm. Their oxidation characteristics of GQDs are listed in Table S1 of Supplementary Information (SI). The oxidized functional group ratios of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and epoxy groups are 1.05:0.9:1.0 ∼ 1.0:1.2:0.96, which is comparable to the experimental value of 1.05:0.9:1.0 ∼ 1.0:1.2:0.96[29,30]. The nanoscale and rectangle-shaped GQDs are studied in terms of current computation ability, as in previous simulation studies[6,7,14]. Even though the shape and size of the mimicked Janus-shaped GQDs differ from the experimental observations reported by Zhang et al. [22], the isolated oxidized nanodomains and pristine graphene nanodomains along the sheet plane, which represent the key feature of the Janus-shaped GQDs, were identified in Fig. 1. The typical simulation setup system is illustrated in Fig. S1 . The lipid bilayer consists of 312 POPC-type lipid molecules with 156 in each leaflet constructed by the Packmol tool[31], with the force field and structure of a single-POPC provided by the Lipidbook server[32]. With the solvent filled, the simulation system has the typical size of 10.0 × 10.0 × 16.0 nm3. GQDs and POPC lipids are described with OPLS-aa, and OPLS-ua forcefields, respectively, while the solvent is modeled with simple point charge (SPC/E) water. Janus-shaped GQDs with different oxidized structures are constructed by using the Polypargen web tool for generating force field parameters and molecular structures[33].

In all MD simulations, Verlet lists are generated using a 1.2 nm neighbor list cutoff. Van der Waals interactions are mimicked using a Lennard-Jones potential within a 1.2 nm cutoff which is smoothed to zero between 1.0 nm and 1.2 nm[34]. Using the smooth Particle Mesh Ewald algorithm with a short-ranged cutoff of 1.2 nm, grid spacing of 0.12 nm, and 4th-order interpolation, electrostatic interactions are calculated. Besides, bonds are constrained using the LINCS algorithm. The steepest descent method with a maximal force of 2.0 × 103 kJ/mol is applied to minimize systems, and the resulting systems are optimized geometrically using a leapfrog integrator at a timestep of 2 fs. By using a V-rescale thermostat, the temperature is kept at 310.0 K with a coupling time of 2.0 ps to mimic physiological temperature. Parrinello-Rhaman pressure couples to 1.0 bar with 2.0 ps coupling time and semi-isotropic compressibility of 4.5 × 10−5 bar−1 [35]. Each simulation is run for 1000.0 ns (or 1.0 µs) under the NPT ensemble. All MD simulations are performed using Gromacs 2020[36] decorated with GPU-accelerated graphics cards and visualization of the simulation results is provided by VMD software[37]. MD simulations are conducted on a multi-node computing platform (four Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 8165@2.10 GHz CPUs and 256 G, one NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU per node). Long-range electrostatic interactions are computed using the CPU, while van der Waals and bonded interactions are computed using the GPU. When compared to pure CPU computation, computing efficiency can be increased by 2.5 ∼ 3.0.

In well-tempered metadynamics (WTmetaD) simulations, a history-dependent bias potential is additionally utilized to enhance the sampling of the free-energy surface based on a predefined set of collective variables (CVs). The history-dependent bias potential
w
WTmetaD

represents the vector of CVs), is a sum of Gaussian hills with width
δ
s
and height
W = ω ·
τ G
centered at the values of the CVs that have already been visited and deposited at the time interval
τ G
with a deposition rate
ω
[38]. Especially, to ensure the convergence of the final bias potential to the actual free-energy, the Gaussian height at each CV position
s
at a time

is typically decreased by a scaling factor:

where the deposition rate,

, and deposition stripe,

, of the Gaussian hills are set to 1.2 kJ/mol and 1.0 ps[39,40], respectively. The bias factor
Δ T
determines the sampling along with the CV space:
Δ T
= 0 signifies the unbiased metadynamics simulations while
Δ T → ∞
indicates non-tempered metadynamics simulations. An estimation error of 10 is set as the bias factor here to ensure good convergence for the free-energy[39,40]. Finally, the free-energy surface can then be computed as follows:
(3)
F

Δ T + T
Δ T
w
WTmetaD
s
, t → ∞

During the transmembrane process, two CVs, the center-of-mass distances between the oxidized/pure graphene nanodomains and a biological membrane are counted. The former identifies the center-of-mass distance between the oxidized nanodomains and the biological membrane, while the latter quantifies the progress of the reaction in a predefined reference path connecting the hydrophobic nanodomains and the biological membrane center. Lastly, the systems are conducted for 1.0 µs with the NPT ensemble. The WTmetaD simulations are conducted with GROMACS 2020 software patched with the PLUMED 2.6 plugin[41]. As estimated from splitting the trajectories and block-averaging, the profiles of standard errors tend to be converging within a range of 2.0 ∼ 2.5 kJ/mol, as simulation time increases, demonstrating that the selected conditions are correctly sampled for these systems[39], as shown in Fig. S2 .

The analysis of dynamical and structural properties of the trajectories such as inter-distance, hydrogen-bond, intermolecular energy, mass density, and solvent-accessible surface areas, is performed with GROMACS 2020 tools using default options. The details of trajectory analysis are included in sections S-I of SI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic equilibrium translation of single-layered GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Using microsecond-long MD trajectories with more than 110 000 atoms, we specifically mimic the translocation protocol of different spatial oxidated GQDs across POPC membranes. The pivotal characteristics of the translocation are analyzed. Fig. 2 A shows GQDs equilibrating in aqueous solutions adhere directly to biological membranes through active binding. After a varying time of loitering at the membrane surface, most of GQDs successfully embed into the biological membrane, except for the severely triangular-oxidized GQDs. A comparison of differential translocation behaviors is shown in Fig. 2B by examining the center-of-mass distance between GQDs and biological membranes. GQDs with low oxidation levels are found to insert deeply and undergo short-lived active binding during the translocation process. Another piece of evidence is found in Fig. 2C, which depicts the evolution of contact numbers between biological membranes and GQDs over time. Generally, the square-oxidized GQDs exhibit a greater penetration than those oxidized by triangular patterns at the same oxidation level.

As reported in our previous work, similar cellular translocation behavior was also found in an all-atom MD simulation of the bio-interface interactions between oxidated GQDs and DPPC bilayer membranes[42]. Despite this, the spatially heterogeneous oxidated GQDs exhibit distinct translocational behavior from the hydrophobic pristine graphene or graphene oxide[6,43]. For instance, owing to the unique structure of separated hydrophobic/hydrophilic nanodomains along the graphene plane, lipid extraction is not observed with the Janus-shaped oxidized GQDs, which induces cytotoxicity through direct damage to the cellular membrane structure[44,45]. As shown in the inset graphic of Fig. 2B, we present two-dimensional number density maps at the interfacial loitering and membrane penetration states. In contrast to the interfacial loitering state, membrane penetration merely results in a caveola followed by low number density in biological membranes, rather than the emergence of noticeable membrane perturbation as observed by conventional oxidized GQDs[46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of cellular translocation at a molecular level
ce:section.ce:para: According to our findings, the identified translocation behavior is majorly driven by two governing factors: i) hydrogen bonding-induced active binding of GQDs from solution onto the membrane surface; ii) hydrophobic association-induced translocation from the membrane surface to the midplane. The time-lapses of the evolution of hydrogen bonds are shown in Fig. 2D1-D2. As the GQDs approach the biological membrane, the number of hydrogen-bindings between them increases dramatically until reaching a plateau. The formation of hydrogen-bonding facilitates GQDs to diffuse fast to the membrane surfaces[47]. Fig. S3 displays the binding configuration of hydrogen bonds between POPC and Janus GQDs, the non-covalent bond sites, including ester-type CO⋯HO (CO), phosphate-type PO⋯HO, are identified. Furthermore, the oxidation levels of GQDs are favorably proportional to equilibrated hydrogen bonds. Compared with square-oxidized GQDs, triangular-oxidized GQDs show a discontinuous tendency in the hydrogen bonds. Despite this, both square-oxidized and triangular-oxidized GQDs conform to similar distance and angle probability distributions of hydrogen bonds, as demonstrated in Fig. 2E1-E2. Besides, the intermolecular interaction energy is also recorded, as presented in Fig. S4 . Whereas both van der Waals and electrostatic interactions contribute to the active binding of GQDs before 0.3 µs, the van der Waals interactions later (>0.3 µs) facilitate the cellular translocation of GQDs. As a result, the van der Waals interactions between POPC lipid tails and hydrophobic graphene usually form an excellent hydrophobic association[11]. Generally, electrostatic interactions drive hydrogen bonds, while van der Waals interactions promote hydrophobic associations. As shown in Fig. S4 , van der Waals energy profiles are close to electrostatic energy at the initial stage (<0.05 µs), which synergistically contributes to the spontaneous and rapid active binding. Fig. S5 depicts the contributions of various energy profiles in the last 0.1 µs, demonstrating the competitiveness in molecular interactions. However, except for severely triangular-oxidized GQDs with active binding, van der Waals energy profiles for all other GQDs are lower than electrostatic energy profiles at the equilibrium stage, which displays deep penetration driven by van der Waals interactions. Thus, from an energy point of perspective, a “competitive coordination” effect to regulate translocation is identified.

We introduce a microsecond-long WTmetaD simulation protocol to quantify the two-dimensional free-energy pathway of GQD translocation across a biological membrane. Two independent CVs are specially adopted to describe the GQD transition from the adsorbed state to inserted state: the center-of-mass distance between oxidized or pure graphene nanodomains and a biological membrane. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the two-dimensional free-energy surface successfully depicts the distinctive landmark regions for the lowly oxidized level of GQD, namely the active binding, and vertical penetration. In fact, the main interactions between GQD and membrane are mainly determined by the strong dispersion interactions between its hydrophobic nanodomains and the lipid tails of the biological membrane[11]. If increasing the oxidation levels of GQDs, the adaptive self-propelling state at the biological membrane surface is observed among the active binding and vertical penetration stages, as shown in Fig. 3B-C. During the active binding stage, the dangling hydrophobic nanodomains rove close to the biological membrane surface, whereas the oxidizing nanodomains are bound by intermolecular hydrogen bonds. At the later adaptive self-propelling state, even though the oxidized nanodomains are anchored through hydrogen-binding, the dangling hydrophobic nanodomains frequently struggle to make contact with the polar lipid heads, causing membrane disruption and allowing the sharp edge to embed into the membrane.

Different from the lowly and moderately oxidized GQDs, the highly oxidized GQDs exhibit two possible translocation pathways, as depicted in Fig. 3C. Though they readily adsorb onto the biological membrane surface, the highly oxidized nanodomains in the GQDs make cellular translocation challenging, as demonstrated by Fig. 2B-C and Fig. 3C (marked by point II). Another low free-energy event occurs, however, under the additional pushing force in a WTmetaD simulation, resulting in successful membrane uptake by overcoming translocation free-energy barriers, as illustrated in Fig. 3C (marked by point II’). Correspondingly, the van Hove correlation function is also calculated to reveal the relevant migration peculiarity. The predominant translocation states are also recognized by the van Hove correlation function, as shown in Fig. 3D-F. Meanwhile, the time evolution of the migration probability of triangular-oxidized GQDs is presented in Fig. S6 . We discover a considerable deviation from the Gaussian distribution. The probability distribution of displacement’s non-Gaussian nature reveals the dynamic heterogeneity of GQDs[48], which validates the staged-translocation behavior of GQDs. According to the aforementioned analyses, we can deduce that moderately Janus-shaped oxidized GQDs have excellent translocation performance, and this is supported by previous experimental results of randomly and moderately oxidized GQDs[17]. Furthermore, the WTmetaD is an effective tool for analyzing sampling free energy landscapes of conformational and chemical transitions, particularly for observing the adaptive self-propelling state that dictates the translocation fate of GQDs[40].

The two-dimensional free-energy surface for various oxidized square-shaped GQDs is depicted in Fig. 4 A-C. Different from triangular-oxidized GQDs, the translocation states of active binding, adaptive self-propelling, and vertical penetration are successively observed as the square-oxidized GQDs gradually embed into the biological membrane matrix. The adaptive self-propelling state improves the GQD entrapment by the biological membrane surface, resulting in efficient membrane penetration. These phased translocations in the two-dimensional free-energy surface are corroborated by the dynamical migration peculiarity of the van Hove correlation function, as displayed in Fig. 4D-F. Similarly, the dynamical heterogeneity of GQDs is further revealed by the non-Gaussian probability distribution of displacement, which explains the several translocation states identified in the two-dimensional free-energy surface. Additionally, the observed emergence of a secondary peak is regarded as an illustration of the hopping process, in which the GQDs hop to the positions previously occupied by one of its nearest neighbors. According to the time evolution of the migration probability of square-oxidized GQDs shown in Fig. S7 , they require a long time to migrate long distances, and thus they are less likely to appear in that limited range at the same time[35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamics comparisons and adaptive self-propelling translocation
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the thermodynamic and dynamical translocation pathways of triangular-oxidized and square-oxidized GQDs, Fig. 5 A1-A2 and 5B1-B2 show the one-dimensional free-energy profiles along with the independent CV as mentioned above. For the hydrophobic nanodomains, both triangular-oxidized and square-oxidized GQDs pass through the energy barrier, indicating an adaptive self-propelling activity along a low free-energy pathway. Square-oxidized GQDs, rather than triangular-oxidized GQDs, continue to exhibit adaptive self-propelling activity in the oxidized nanodomains, indicating that square-oxidized GQDs are more capable of self-propelling translocation. This is due to the “solvent-retention” effects resulting from the large amounts of solvent-accessible surface areas found in triangular-oxidized GQDs[49], as shown in Fig. 5C. Meanwhile, low membrane perturbation is observed when square-oxidized GQDs are compared to triangular-oxidized GQDs, as illustrated by the two-dimensional number density in Fig. S8 . Besides, to quantify the adaptive self-propelling activity, the typical real-time trajectories of relatively spatial mobile for different nanodomains are presented in Fig. 5D1-D4. The oxidized nanodomains locally migrate at the membrane surface, whereas the hydrophobic nanodomains are highly mobile as they strive to become embedded into the biological membrane at a deeper level. The hydrophobic/hydrophilic nanodomains are spatially isolated and function as anchors and pumps, respectively, then being competitively coordinated for the adaptive self-propelling translocation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Translocation pathway and cytotoxicity of multi-layered GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we systematically construct the equilibrium states in the biological membrane translocation of Janus-shaped GQDs as a function of spatially heterogeneous oxidation, oxidation level, and nanoflake thickness, as shown in Fig. 6 A. The corresponding dynamical pathways of multilayered GQDs are included in Figs. S9-S12 . In the phase diagram, three characteristic regions corresponding to the representative snapshots of Fig. 6B1-B4 can be identified, which have been approximately marked by different patterns. Under the same conditions, the dynamics simulation for 1.0 µs is performed to confirm the state of each point in the phase diagram. As shown in Fig. S13 , the multilayered GQDs are discovered to be stably self-assembled colloidal particles during the translocation process based on their radius-of-gyration and contact number[17]. As denoted by the red circles, the lateral insertion ranges with a nanoflake layer no larger than about 2 and displays a very low oxidation level. In this case, the hydrophobic interaction between the lipid tails and the hydrophobic nanodomains regulates the dynamic process, and they stimulate GQDs to permeate the bilayer center spontaneously via adaptive insertion and spatial rotation. When the oxidation level exceeds 10.06 %, the translocational state switches to vertical insertion, which is fueled primarily and simultaneously by hydrogen-bindings between graphene nanodomains and polar lipid heads, as well as hydrophobic-associations between graphene nanodomains and nonpolar lipid tails. The blue squares clarify that the active binding states occur for almost all types of high-thickness GQDs whereas one can still identify the subtle difference between high thickness and high oxidation levels that all induce the active binding. Notably, the translocation of multilayered GQDs is impeded by accumulative synergy-induced steric hindrance and hydrophilic attractions of lipid heads with the oxidized nanodomains[17].

The cytotoxicity of GQDs is dependent on their translocational states, as shown by the mass density profiles and electrostatic potentials in Fig. 6C1-C2. The hydrophobic nanodomains of GQDs dynamically neutralize at the lipid-tail interface, while their hydrophilic nanodomains expose to solvent. Further, the asymmetric electrostatic potential profiles demonstrate electrostatic neutralization (or complementarity) between biological membranes and GQDs, which is correlated with the oxidation levels of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6D1-D4, the two-dimensional number density distribution can also quantify the membrane perturbation. Increasing the thickness of the nanoflake or embedding translocational states causes more severe structural changes in the biological membrane matrix: mechanical deformation of biological membranes is exacerbated by adsorption-induced perturbation, whereas structural defects are caused by penetration-induced perturbation. The two-dimensional pressure tensor distribution within the cellular membranes is shown in Fig. 6E1-E4, which demonstrates different degrees of mechanically heterogeneous distribution and membrane deformation around the GQDs. Although three equilibrium translocation states are observed (see Fig. 6B1-B4), namely lateral insertion, vertical insertion, and active binding, the resultant membrane perturbation differs. Based on the number density distribution and pressure tensor, the membrane perturbation caused by lateral or vertical insertion is more severe than that caused by active binding. Besides, Figs. S14-S15 depict the intermolecular interaction energy, which includes the van der Waals and electrostatic interactions. Based on the interaction energy profiles and phase diagram, it appears that utilizing the adaptive rotation of GQDs can alter the perturbation of cellular membranes. When the inter-distance between GQDs and biological membranes reaches a critical threshold value, depending on the translation states, the membrane deforms to an energetically favorable state by releasing nanomechanical and chemical energy through membrane rearrangement or deformation. Compared with graphene oxide, Janus-shaped GQDs prefer to translocate into biological membranes; however, compared to pristine graphene, they exhibit weak membrane perturbation rather than lipid extraction[17]. Thus, the identified adaptive translocational states of GQDs are driven by intermolecular mechanochemistry[50,51], which enables them to undergo low free-energy barriers and minimal membrane perturbation during the translocation process.

Herein, using microsecond-long MD simulations and WTmetaD simulations, we unravel the dynamic and thermodynamic properties of Janus-shaped GQDs as they spontaneously translocate through the biological membrane. The spatially heterogeneous oxidation and nanoflake thickness are analyzed. Janus-shaped GQDs operate as self-propelled nanomotors, with the aid of intermolecular interactions, such as hydrogen-binding between oxidation nanodomains and polar lipid heads, and hydrophobic associations between graphene nanodomains and lipid tails. The phase diagram is drawn as a function of spatial oxidation pattern, oxidation degree, and nanoflake thickness. Moderately oxidized GQDs show good cellular translocation activities both dynamically and thermodynamically because of the “competitive coordination” effect. Equilibrium MD simulations identify active recognition, adaptive self-propelling state, and insertion/uptake; specifically, the adaptive self-propelling activity is captured by the two-dimensional free-energy landscape.

Triangular-oxidized GQDs exhibit weaker translocation than square-oxidized GQDs due to the “solvation-retention” effect resulting from the heterogeneous redistribution in oxidated nanodomains. Furthermore, the cellular translocation of multilayered GQDs is impeded by accumulative synergy-induced steric hindrance. Intermolecular mechanochemistry is found to play a crucial role in energy transduction and nanomechanical interactions, which affect the spatial rotation pathways of GQDs as well as membrane structural perturbations. In this case, a high degree of cellular toxicity is estimated, as evidenced by their seriously heterogeneous distribution in membrane thickness and mechanical tensor. Overall, spatially heterogeneous oxidation can be exploited as a self-propelled strategy to successfully translocate Janus-shaped GQDs through biological membranes with a low free-energy barrier and minimal membrane perturbation. The biomedical field should further prioritize the precise regulation of the self-propelled motion strength and directionality of the translocating GQDs[49]. Besides, the synergistic effects of oxidized functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl, ether, and carboxyl groups) and cellular membrane composition on Janus-shape GQD translocation, in terms of internalization mechanism and cytotoxicity, are particularly crucial to analyze in the future[7].

Xianyu Song: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Hongchao Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xianli Duan: Investigation. Qi Hu: Investigation. Kezhong Liang: Investigation. Tingzhen Li: Supervision. Shuangliang Zhao: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Honglai Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22108022, and 21878078), and the Guangxi Science and Technology Base and Talent Project (AD21220017), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0005), the Chongqing Doctoral “Through Train” Scientific Research Project (No. CSTB2022BSXM-JCX0133), and the Science and Technology Innovation Program of Wanzhou (No. wzstc20210310).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155425.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
